case_id: Brown v. Board of Education (1954 and 1955)

pi_items.source: Supreme Court records and multiple legal databases (Justia, Cornell Law, Library of Congress). Brown v. Board of Education I was decided May 17, 1954.
U.S. Reports citation 347 U.S. 483 (1954). Confirmed via Justia, Cornell Law LII, Library of Congress, and FindLaw.
U.S. Reports citation 347 U.S. 483 (1954). Official citation confirmed across all major legal databases.

pi_items.metadata_value: Brown v. Board of Education II
pi_items.verified_value: Brown v. Board of Education of Topeka (colloquially 'Brown II' or 'Brown v. Board of Education II')
pi_items.source: The official case name in U.S. Reports is 'Brown v. Board of Education of Topeka' for both the 1954 and 1955 decisions. However, 'Brown II' is the standard colloquial name used to distinguish the 1955 implementation decree from the original 1954 decision. Web search results from Quimbee, UMKC, and other legal sources confirm this naming convention.
Supreme Court records confirm Brown v. Board of Education II was decided May 31, 1955. Confirmed via Justia, National Archives, and legal databases.
U.S. Reports citation 349 U.S. 294 (1955). Official citation for the 1955 decision confirmed across all major legal databases.
U.S. Reports citation 349 U.S. 294 (1955). Confirmed via Justia, Cornell Law LII, Library of Congress, and FindLaw.

Brown II (1955) was an implementation decree requiring desegregation 'with all deliberate speed'. Web search results from Supreme Court Historical Society, National Archives, Smithsonian, and multiple law review articles confirm all three keywords are central to the Brown II decision's content and intent.

privileged_info_summary: The task involves real historical legal cases from the U.S. Supreme Court: Brown v. Board of Education I (347 U.S. 483, decided May 17, 1954) declared racial segregation in public schools unconstitutional. Brown v. Board of Education II (349 U.S. 294, decided May 31, 1955) is the implementation decree requiring desegregation of public schools with all deliberate speed. All metadata about case citations (volume, page, year) and the core keywords (implementation, desegregation, deliberate speed) are accurate and verified against official U.S. Supreme Court reports and authoritative legal databases.
